sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Ten — writing linear equations. Last module we measured how steep a line was. Today we learn to write the equation that describes the line itself.

Imagine a gym charges you a thirty dollar join fee plus fifteen dollars a month. Can you predict what you'll have paid by month six? By month twelve? By month thirty? You could grab a calculator each time, or you could write one equation that handles any month forever. Two facts in — infinite predictions out. That's the power of a linear equation.

Three ways to write a line. First, when you have the slope and the y-intercept. Second, when you have a slope and any single point. Third, when you only have two points. Then we'll handle parallel and perpendicular lines as a bonus. By the end, any combination of facts about a line — you'll be able to write its equation.

Quick recap. Slope-intercept form is y equals m x plus b. The m is the slope — how steep. The b is the y-intercept — where the line crosses the y-axis. Two letters, two pieces of information, and you have the whole line.

Easiest case first. Slope is four, y-intercept is negative one. Just plug into the form. m becomes four, b becomes negative one, and the equation is y equals four x minus one. That's it. When you're given slope and y-intercept, it's a straight plug-in.

Now what if you have a slope but not the y-intercept? Use point-slope form. y minus y one equals m times the quantity x minus x one. The y one and x one are just the coordinates of any point on the line. You drop those numbers in, drop in your slope, and you've got a working equation.

Example. Slope is three, the line passes through the point two comma seven. Plug into point-slope: y minus seven equals three times the quantity x minus two. Distribute the three: y minus seven equals three x minus six. Add seven to both sides: y equals three x plus one. Clean slope-intercept form, done.

What if you only have two points? Step one: find the slope. Step two: use point-slope. Points are one comma two and three comma eight. Slope equals eight minus two over three minus one — that's three. Now use either point — let's take one comma two: y minus two equals three times the quantity x minus one. Simplify: y equals three x minus one.
Your turn. Write the equation of the line through two comma five and four comma eleven. Hint: find the slope first, then use point-slope. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Time. Slope first — eleven minus five over four minus two equals six over two equals three. Now use the point two comma five: y minus five equals three times the quantity x minus two. Distribute: y minus five equals three x minus six. Add five: y equals three x minus one. If you got it — well done. If not, redo it slowly and you'll lock it in.

Parallel lines. Find the line through three comma one that's parallel to y equals two x plus five. Parallel lines have the same slope — so our slope is two. Use point-slope with three comma one: y minus one equals two times the quantity x minus three. Distribute and simplify: y equals two x minus five. Same slope as the original, different y-intercept — they'll never cross.

Perpendicular lines. Same point three comma one, but now perpendicular to y equals two x plus five. Perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal — flip and change the sign — so our slope is negative one half. Point-slope: y minus one equals negative one half times the quantity x minus three. Simplify: y equals negative one half x plus five halves. The two lines now cross at a perfect ninety degree angle.

Back to the gym. Thirty dollar join fee, fifteen dollars a month. That join fee is your starting cost — your y-intercept, thirty. The monthly fifteen is your slope — the rate per month. So the equation is y equals fifteen x plus thirty. Month six? Fifteen times six plus thirty equals one hundred twenty dollars. Month twelve? Fifteen times twelve plus thirty equals two hundred ten dollars. One equation, infinite predictions.

Two special cases worth knowing. A horizontal line has zero slope — it's just y equals some number. y equals four is a flat line at height four. A vertical line has undefined slope and can't be written in slope-intercept form at all. It's just x equals some number — like x equals negative two. Easy to spot, easy to write.

Recap. Slope-intercept form when you have slope and y-intercept. Point-slope form when you have a slope and any point. Two points — find slope first, then use point-slope. Parallel lines share the slope. Perpendicular lines have negative-reciprocal slopes. Your assignment is in the dashboard — write five equations from different combinations of inputs. Next module we'll start combining equations into systems.

Here's how to actually master this. The video is about ten percent of the work. Read the OpenStax chapter on finding equations of lines. Do the Khan Academy practice on writing linear equations. Submit your dashboard assignment. And if anything feels fuzzy, book a quick advisor check-in. Next up: Module Eleven — systems of equations. See you there.
